message.content: read the bug we described in @installation-bug.txt . See @temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/ reference that also generate IDE specfic configurations.

content.thinking:
The user wants me to read the installation bug file and look at how the BMAD-METHOD reference generates IDE-specific configurations, so I can understand what's missing in the standalone SKF installer.

The bug file has already been read (shown in the system reminder). The key issue is:

> "As you will notice, only the Method 2 also generated IDE specific configurations. For example, it also generated the commands for the selected tools/IDEs Claude and Cursor. Our standalone method need improvment."

So the standalone `npx bmad-module-skill-forge install` (Method 1) doesn't generate IDE-specific configurations (like `.claude/commands/` or `.cursor/` rules), while installing via `npx bmad-method install` (Method 2) does.


I need to examine the BMAD-METHOD source code to see how it handles IDE configuration generation, then compare that against the SKF standalone installer to identify what's missing.

content.text:
I've read the bug file. The issue is clear: **the standalone SKF installer (Method 1) doesn't generate IDE-specific configurations** (e.g., Claude Code commands, Cursor rules), while installing via `npx bmad-method install` (Method 2) does.

Let me explore the BMAD-METHOD source to understand how it generates IDE configs, and compare with the current SKF installer.

content.thinking: Let me explore both the BMAD-METHOD source for how it generates IDE configs, the demo output directories, and the current SKF installer code.

content.text: Let me explore the BMAD-METHOD source for IDE config generation and compare the demo outputs.

   214→# ============================================================================
   215→# Installer Config Schema
   216→# ============================================================================
   217→#
   218→# installer:
   219→#   target_dir: string                    # Directory where artifacts are installed
   220→#   template_type: string                 # Default template type to use
   221→#   header_template: string (optional)    # Override for header/frontmatter template
   222→#   body_template: string (optional)      # Override for body/content template
   223→#   legacy_targets: array (optional)      # Old target dirs to clean up on reinstall (migration)
   224→#     - string                            # Relative path, e.g. .opencode/agent
   225→#   targets: array (optional)             # For multi-target installations
   226→#     - target_dir: string
   227→#       template_type: string
   228→#       artifact_types: [agents, workflows, tasks, tools]
   229→#   artifact_types: array (optional)      # Filter which artifacts to install (default: all)
   230→#   skip_existing: boolean (optional)     # Skip files that already exist (default: false)
   231→#   skill_format: boolean (optional)   # Use directory-per-skill output: <name>/SKILL.md
   232→#                                     # with clean frontmatter (name + description, unquoted)
   233→#   ancestor_conflict_check: boolean (optional) # Refuse install when ancestor dir has BMAD files
   234→#                                                # in the same target_dir (for IDEs that inherit
   235→#                                                # skills from parent directories)
   236→
   237→# ============================================================================
   238→# Platform Categories
   239→# ============================================================================
   240→
   241→categories:
   242→  ide:
   243→    name: "Integrated Development Environment"
   244→    description: "Full-featured code editors with AI assistance"
   245→
   246→  cli:
   247→    name: "Command Line Interface"
   248→    description: "Terminal-based tools"
   249→
   250→  tool:
   251→    name: "Development Tool"
   252→    description: "Standalone development utilities"
   253→
   254→  service:
   255→    name: "Cloud Service"
   256→    description: "Cloud-based development platforms"
   257→
   258→  extension:
   259→    name: "Editor Extension"
   260→    description: "Plugins for existing editors"
   261→
   262→# ============================================================================
   263→# Naming Conventions and Rules
   264→# ============================================================================
   265→
   266→conventions:
   267→  code_format: "lowercase-kebab-case"
   268→  name_format: "Title Case"
   269→  max_code_length: 20
   270→  allowed_characters: "a-z0-9-"

Excellent! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the system. Let me create a detailed report:

## BMAD-METHOD IDE Configuration Generation System - Deep Analysis

I've thoroughly explored the BMAD-METHOD source code and identified the comprehensive system for generating IDE-specific configurations. Here's the complete technical analysis:

### **Key Architecture Overview**

The system uses a **config-driven pipeline** with three main components:

1. **IDE Manager** - Dynamic handler discovery and orchestration
2. **Command Generators** - Artifact-specific content generation
3. **Platform Configuration** - Centralized IDE/tool definitions

---

### **Files and Logic Flow**

#### **1. Core Configuration File**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/platform-codes.yaml`

This is the **central configuration** that defines all 20+ supported IDEs:
- Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot
- Kilo, Rovo Dev, Codex, OpenCode, Antigravity
- Gemini, Auggie, CodeBuddy, Crush, iFlow, Kiro, Qwen, Roo, Trae, and more

Each IDE entry specifies:
- **target_dir**: Where to install files (e.g., `.claude/skills`, `.cursor/skills`)
- **template_type**: Template system to use (default, windsurf, kiro, gemini, etc.)
- **skill_format**: Boolean flag for directory-per-skill output format
- **artifact_types**: Which artifact types to install (agents, workflows, tasks, tools)
- **legacy_targets**: Old directories to clean up during reinstall
- **ancestor_conflict_check**: Whether to check parent directories for conflicts

#### **2. IDE Manager - Dynamic Handler Loading**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/manager.js`

This orchestrates all IDE setup operations:
- **loadHandlers()** loads handlers in two phases:
  1. Custom installers: github-copilot.js, kilo.js, rovodev.js
  2. Config-driven handlers: created from platform-codes.yaml for remaining IDEs
- **setup()** method: Calls appropriate handler for each IDE
- **detectInstalledIdes()**: Discovers which IDEs are already configured
- **cleanupByList()**: Removes configurations during uninstall

#### **3. Base IDE Setup Class**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/_base-ide.js`

The foundation class that all IDE handlers extend:

**Key Methods:**
- **getAgents()**: Scans bmad directory for all agents (core, module, standalone)
- **getWorkflows()**: Finds workflow.yaml files recursively
- **getTasks()** / **getTools()**: Collects task/tool artifacts
- **processContent()**: Replaces placeholders like {project-root}, {module}, {agent}
- **createAgentConfig()**: Generates agent configuration files

**Artifact Collection Logic:**
```
Agents:
  - bmad/core/agents/
  - bmad/{module}/agents/
  - bmad/agents/{folder}/ (standalone)

Workflows:
  - Recursively find workflow.yaml files
  - Parse for name, description, standalone flag

Tasks/Tools:
  - bmad/core/tasks/ and bmad/core/tools/
  - bmad/{module}/tasks/ and bmad/{module}/tools/
  - Filter by standalone attribute
```

#### **4. Config-Driven IDE Setup**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/_config-driven.js`

Handles standard IDE installations based on platform-codes.yaml:

**Key Methods:**
- **installToTarget()**: Single target directory installation
- **installToMultipleTargets()**: GitHub Copilot uses this (multiple .github/agents, .github/prompts)
- **writeAgentArtifacts()**: Renders and writes agent commands
- **writeWorkflowArtifacts()**: Handles YAML vs MD workflow templates
- **writeTaskToolArtifacts()**: Writes task and tool commands

**Template Rendering Logic:**
- Loads appropriate template based on template_type from platform-codes.yaml
- Falls back to default templates if specific ones don't exist
- Uses skill_format flag to choose between:
  - **Directory-per-skill**: `{target_dir}/bmad-agent-name/SKILL.md`
  - **Flat**: `{target_dir}/bmad-agent-name.md`

#### **5. Command Generators**

**Agent Command Generator**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/shared/agent-command-generator.js`

- Collects agents from all modules
- Generates launcher content from template
- Replaces {{name}}, {{module}}, {{path}}, {{description}}

**Workflow Command Generator**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/shared/workflow-command-generator.js`

- Loads workflow manifest CSV
- Distinguishes YAML vs Markdown workflows
- Uses different templates based on workflow type

**Task/Tool Command Generator**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/shared/task-tool-command-generator.js`

- Collects task and tool artifacts from manifest
- Filters by artifact_types config
- Handles template rendering for each artifact

#### **6. Custom IDE Handlers (Special Cases)**

**GitHub Copilot**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/github-copilot.js`

- Creates .github/agents/ and .github/prompts/ directories
- Generates .agent.md files from agent artifacts
- Creates copilot-instructions.md with BMAD-specific instructions
- Handles tool permissions from bmad-help.csv
- **Multi-target approach**: agents in one dir, prompts in another

**Kilo** (kilo.js) and **Rovo Dev** (rovodev.js): Custom installers for unique platforms

#### **7. IDE Configuration Persistence**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/core/ide-config-manager.js`

Saves IDE-specific settings to: `bmad/_config/ides/{ideName}.yaml`
- Preserves configured_date across updates
- Tracks last_updated timestamp
- Allows reusing saved configurations on reinstall

#### **8. Path Transformation Utilities**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/shared/path-utils.js`

Converts hierarchical paths to flat names:
- **Dash format (new standard)**: `bmad-agent-module-name.md`
- **Legacy underscore format**: `bmad_agent_module_name.md`
- **Colon format aliases**: Backward compatibility

Examples:
- `bmm/agents/pm.md` → `bmad-agent-bmm-pm.md`
- `core/agents/brainstorming.md` → `bmad-agent-brainstorming.md`
- `bmm/workflows/correct-course.md` → `bmad-bmm-correct-course.md`

#### **9. Template System**
**Files:**
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/templates/agent-command-template.md`
- `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/ide/templates/workflow-command-template.md`

**Agent Template** includes:
- YAML frontmatter with name and description
- XML activation block with agent loading instructions
- References {project-root}/{module}/agents/{path}

**Workflow Template** includes:
- Description frontmatter
- Steps to load core workflow.xml
- Parameter passing for specific workflow config

#### **10. Installation Flow Integration**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/core/installer.js`

Main installer calls IDE setup after manifest generation:

```javascript
// Around line 1208
const setupResult = await this.ideManager.setup(ide, projectDir, bmadDir, {
  selectedModules: allModules || [],
  preCollectedConfig: ideConfigurations[ide] || null,
  verbose: config.verbose,
  silent: ideHasConfig,
});
```

Workflow:
1. **ensureInitialized()**: Load all IDE handlers
2. **setup()**: Call handler for each selected IDE
3. **saveIdeConfig()**: Persist configuration to disk
4. Results aggregated and displayed to user

#### **11. Manifest Generator**
**File:** `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/temp/BMAD-METHOD-main/tools/cli/installers/lib/core/manifest-generator.js`

Generates manifest CSVs that command generators use:
- Scans all installed modules
- Collects agents, workflows, tasks, tools
- Creates CSV indices for fast lookup
- Marks standalone vs internal artifacts

---

### **Data Flow Diagram**

```
platform-codes.yaml
    ↓
IdeManager.loadHandlers()
    ├── Custom installers (github-copilot.js, kilo.js, rovodev.js)
    └── ConfigDrivenIdeSetup (from yaml config)
         ↓
    setup(ideName, projectDir, bmadDir)
         ↓
    AgentCommandGenerator.collectAgentArtifacts()
    WorkflowCommandGenerator.collectWorkflowArtifacts()
    TaskToolCommandGenerator.collectTaskToolArtifacts()
         ↓
    renderTemplate(template, artifact)
         ↓
    writeAgentArtifacts() / writeWorkflowArtifacts() / writeTaskToolArtifacts()
         ↓
    {target_dir}/bmad-{name}.md (flat)
    OR
    {target_dir}/bmad-{name}/SKILL.md (skill_format)
         ↓
    IdeConfigManager.saveIdeConfig() → bmad/_config/ides/{ide}.yaml
```

---

### **Key Design Patterns**

1. **Config-Driven**: Platform definitions in YAML, handlers generated automatically
2. **Template-Based**: Reusable templates with variable substitution
3. **Hierarchical to Flat**: Nested module paths → dash-separated filenames
4. **Artifact Filtering**: Each IDE specifies which artifact types it supports
5. **Multi-Target**: Support for IDEs with multiple installation targets
6. **Custom Override**: Special cases (Copilot, Kilo, Rovo) with custom logic
7. **Persistence**: IDE configs saved to disk for reuse across reinstalls
8. **Modular**: Each IDE type gets dedicated command generator

---

### **Summary of Key Files**

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| platform-codes.yaml | Central IDE/platform configuration definitions |
| manager.js | IDE handler discovery & orchestration |
| _base-ide.js | Base class for all IDE handlers |
| _config-driven.js | Config-based IDE setup (20+ IDEs) |
| agent-command-generator.js | Agent artifact collection & rendering |
| workflow-command-generator.js | Workflow artifact collection |
| task-tool-command-generator.js | Task/tool artifact collection |
| github-copilot.js | Custom GitHub Copilot handler |
| ide-config-manager.js | IDE configuration persistence |
| path-utils.js | Hierarchical → flat path transformation |
| installer.js | Main orchestrator that calls IDE setup |

This system elegantly scales to support 20+ IDEs with minimal custom code, relying on configuration and templates rather than per-IDE boilerplate.

cat > /tmp/summary.txt << 'EOF'
COMPREHENSIVE COMPARISON OF THREE INSTALLATION METHODS
=====================================================

INSTALLATION METHOD 1: fast/ (Standalone SKF)
- .claude directory: ABSENT
- .cursor directory: ABSENT
- IDE-specific commands: NONE
- _bmad structure: /fast/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/*
- _bmad/_config: ABSENT
- _bmad/core: ABSENT
- .cursorrules files: NONE

INSTALLATION METHOD 2: quick/ (bmad-method WITH integrated SKF)
- .claude directory: PRESENT
  * Contains: .claude/commands/ with 21 command files
  * Includes 11 SKF-specific command files (bmad-skf-*)
  * Includes 1 SKF agent command (bmad-agent-skf-skf.md)
- .cursor directory: PRESENT  
  * Contains: .cursor/commands/ with 21 command files (identical to .claude)
  * Includes 11 SKF-specific command files (bmad-skf-*)
  * Includes 1 SKF agent command (bmad-agent-skf-skf.md)
- IDE-specific commands: 21 total (11 SKF + 10 core/BMAD)
- _bmad structure:
  * /quick/_bmad/_config/ (BMAD-specific configuration directory)
  * /quick/_bmad/core/ (BMAD core workflows/agents)
  * /quick/_bmad/skf/ (SKF module with workflows)
  * SKF workflows: /quick/_bmad/skf/workflows/* (NOT under /skillforge/)
  * /quick/_bmad/_config/custom/skf/ (Custom SKF configuration)
  * /quick/_bmad/_config/ides/ (IDE configuration YAML files)
- .cursorrules files: NONE

INSTALLATION METHOD 3: deep/ (bmad-method WITHOUT SKF initially, then SKF added)
- .claude directory: PRESENT
  * Contains: .claude/commands/ with 10 command files (CORE ONLY)
  * NO SKF-specific command files initially
- .cursor directory: PRESENT
  * Contains: .cursor/commands/ with 10 command files (CORE ONLY)
  * NO SKF-specific command files initially
- IDE-specific commands: 10 total (CORE/BMAD only, no SKF commands)
- _bmad structure:
  * /deep/_bmad/_config/ (BMAD-specific configuration directory)
  * /deep/_bmad/core/ (BMAD core workflows/agents)
  * /deep/_bmad/skf/ (SKF module with workflows)
  * /deep/_bmad/_memory/ (Ferris sidecar memory)
  * SKF workflows: /deep/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/* (under /skillforge/)
  * /deep/_bmad/_config/ides/ (IDE configuration YAML files)
  * NO /deep/_bmad/_config/custom/skf/ (no custom SKF config)
- .cursorrules files: NONE

KEY DIFFERENCES IN IDE-SPECIFIC CONFIGURATIONS:
================================================

1. CLI COMMAND FILES (.claude/commands and .cursor/commands):
   - Method 1 (fast): NO command files generated
   - Method 2 (quick): 21 command files (includes 11 SKF + 10 core commands)
   - Method 3 (deep): 10 command files (ONLY core commands, NO SKF commands)
   
   NOTE: Method 2 has 11 SKF-specific commands that Method 3 lacks:
   - bmad-agent-skf-skf.md
   - bmad-skf-analyze-source.md
   - bmad-skf-audit-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-brief-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-create-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-create-stack-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-export-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-quick-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-setup-forge.md
   - bmad-skf-test-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-update-skill.md

2. _BMAD DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
   - Method 1 (fast): 
     * Minimal: /skf/, /agents/, /knowledge/, /workflows/skillforge/
     * NO _config directory
     * Workflows nested under /skillforge/
   
   - Method 2 (quick) - BMAD-INTEGRATED:
     * Has /_config/ (BMAD configuration hub)
     * Has /_config/custom/skf/ (Custom SKF configuration overlays)
     * Has /_config/ides/ (IDE configuration files)
     * SKF workflows NOT under /skillforge/ (flat structure)
     * Includes /core/ with BMAD-specific workflows
   
   - Method 3 (deep) - BMAD-INTEGRATED but SKF ADDED LATER:
     * Has /_config/ (BMAD configuration hub)
     * NO /_config/custom/skf/ (SKF was added after, not integrated)
     * Has /_config/ides/ (IDE configuration files)
     * SKF workflows ARE under /skillforge/ (separate namespace)
     * Includes /core/ with BMAD-specific workflows
     * Includes /_memory/ for Ferris sidecar

3. .CLAUDE AND .CURSOR DIRECTORIES:
   - Method 1 (fast): NONE (not created)
   - Method 2 (quick): PRESENT with 21 command files
   - Method 3 (deep): PRESENT with only 10 command files (SKF commands missing)

4. IDE CONFIGURATION FILES:
   - Method 1 (fast): NO ides/ directory
   - Method 2 (quick): _bmad/_config/ides/claude-code.yaml and cursor.yaml
   - Method 3 (deep): _bmad/_config/ides/claude-code.yaml and cursor.yaml

CRITICAL INSIGHT: What Method 2 Generates That Method 1 Doesn't
==============================================================

Method 2 (bmad-method WITH SKF) generates the following IDE-specific files that 
Method 1 (standalone SKF) does NOT:

1. IDE COMMAND FILES (complete integration):
   - 21 total command files in .claude/commands/ and .cursor/commands/
   - 11 SKF-specific commands registered with the IDE
   - 10 core BMAD commands registered with the IDE

2. BMAD CONFIGURATION INFRASTRUCTURE:
   - _bmad/_config/ directory with manifests and agent configs
   - _bmad/_config/custom/skf/ with SKF customization overlays
   - _bmad/_config/ides/ with IDE-specific configuration YAML files

3. INTEGRATED COMMAND DISCOVERY:
   - Both .claude and .cursor directories with identical command trees
   - IDE-agnostic command registration system
   - Integrated agent manifest and task manifest
EOF

- .cursor directory: ABSENT
- IDE-specific commands: NONE
- _bmad structure: /fast/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/*
- _bmad/_config: ABSENT
- _bmad/core: ABSENT
- .cursorrules files: NONE

INSTALLATION METHOD 2: quick/ (bmad-method WITH integrated SKF)
- .claude directory: PRESENT
  * Contains: .claude/commands/ with 21 command files
  * Includes 11 SKF-specific command files (bmad-skf-*)
  * Includes 1 SKF agent command (bmad-agent-skf-skf.md)
- .cursor directory: PRESENT  
  * Contains: .cursor/commands/ with 21 command files (identical to .claude)
  * Includes 11 SKF-specific command files (bmad-skf-*)
  * Includes 1 SKF agent command (bmad-agent-skf-skf.md)
- IDE-specific commands: 21 total (11 SKF + 10 core/BMAD)
- _bmad structure:
  * /quick/_bmad/_config/ (BMAD-specific configuration directory)
  * /quick/_bmad/core/ (BMAD core workflows/agents)
  * /quick/_bmad/skf/ (SKF module with workflows)
  * SKF workflows: /quick/_bmad/skf/workflows/* (NOT under /skillforge/)
  * /quick/_bmad/_config/custom/skf/ (Custom SKF configuration)
  * /quick/_bmad/_config/ides/ (IDE configuration YAML files)
- .cursorrules files: NONE

INSTALLATION METHOD 3: deep/ (bmad-method WITHOUT SKF initially, then SKF added)
- .claude directory: PRESENT
  * Contains: .claude/commands/ with 10 command files (CORE ONLY)
  * NO SKF-specific command files initially
- .cursor directory: PRESENT
  * Contains: .cursor/commands/ with 10 command files (CORE ONLY)
  * NO SKF-specific command files initially
- IDE-specific commands: 10 total (CORE/BMAD only, no SKF commands)
- _bmad structure:
  * /deep/_bmad/_config/ (BMAD-specific configuration directory)
  * /deep/_bmad/core/ (BMAD core workflows/agents)
  * /deep/_bmad/skf/ (SKF module with workflows)
  * /deep/_bmad/_memory/ (Ferris sidecar memory)
  * SKF workflows: /deep/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/* (under /skillforge/)
  * /deep/_bmad/_config/ides/ (IDE configuration YAML files)
  * NO /deep/_bmad/_config/custom/skf/ (no custom SKF config)
- .cursorrules files: NONE

KEY DIFFERENCES IN IDE-SPECIFIC CONFIGURATIONS:
================================================

1. CLI COMMAND FILES (.claude/commands and .cursor/commands):
   - Method 1 (fast): NO command files generated
   - Method 2 (quick): 21 command files (includes 11 SKF + 10 core commands)
   - Method 3 (deep): 10 command files (ONLY core commands, NO SKF commands)
   
   NOTE: Method 2 has 11 SKF-specific commands that Method 3 lacks:
   - bmad-agent-skf-skf.md
   - bmad-skf-analyze-source.md
   - bmad-skf-audit-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-brief-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-create-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-create-stack-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-export-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-quick-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-setup-forge.md
   - bmad-skf-test-skill.md
   - bmad-skf-update-skill.md

2. _BMAD DIRECTORY STRUCTURE:
   - Method 1 (fast): 
     * Minimal: /skf/, /agents/, /knowledge/, /workflows/skillforge/
     * NO _config directory
     * Workflows nested under /skillforge/
   
   - Method 2 (quick) - BMAD-INTEGRATED:
     * Has /_config/ (BMAD configuration hub)
     * Has /_config/custom/skf/ (Custom SKF configuration overlays)
     * Has /_config/ides/ (IDE configuration files)
     * SKF workflows NOT under /skillforge/ (flat structure)
     * Includes /core/ with BMAD-specific workflows
   
   - Method 3 (deep) - BMAD-INTEGRATED but SKF ADDED LATER:
     * Has /_config/ (BMAD configuration hub)
     * NO /_config/custom/skf/ (SKF was added after, not integrated)
     * Has /_config/ides/ (IDE configuration files)
     * SKF workflows ARE under /skillforge/ (separate namespace)
     * Includes /core/ with BMAD-specific workflows
     * Includes /_memory/ for Ferris sidecar

3. .CLAUDE AND .CURSOR DIRECTORIES:
   - Method 1 (fast): NONE (not created)
   - Method 2 (quick): PRESENT with 21 command files
   - Method 3 (deep): PRESENT with only 10 command files (SKF commands missing)

4. IDE CONFIGURATION FILES:
   - Method 1 (fast): NO ides/ directory
   - Method 2 (quick): _bmad/_config/ides/claude-code.yaml and cursor.yaml
   - Method 3 (deep): _bmad/_config/ides/claude-code.yaml and cursor.yaml

CRITICAL INSIGHT: What Method 2 Generates That Method 1 Doesn't
==============================================================

Method 2 (bmad-method WITH SKF) generates the following IDE-specific files that 
Method 1 (standalone SKF) does NOT:

1. IDE COMMAND FILES (complete integration):

   - 11 SKF-specific commands registered with the IDE

2. BMAD CONFIGURATION INFRASTRUCTURE:
   - _bmad/_config/ directory with manifests and agent configs
   - _bmad/_config/custom/skf/ with SKF customization overlays
   - _bmad/_config/ides/ with IDE-specific configuration YAML files

3. INTEGRATED COMMAND DISCOVERY:
   - Both .claude and .cursor directories with identical command trees
   - IDE-agnostic command registration system
   - Integrated agent manifest and task manifest

## IDE-SPECIFIC CONFIGURATION COMPARISON: Three Installation Methods

Based on my analysis of the three demo directories, here are the exact differences in IDE-specific configurations:

### SUMMARY TABLE

| Aspect | Method 1 (fast/) | Method 2 (quick/) | Method 3 (deep/) |

| **IDE config files** | 0 | 2 | 2 |

### KEY DIFFERENCES

#### 1. IDE COMMAND FILES (.claude/commands and .cursor/commands)

**Method 1 (Standalone SKF - fast/):**
- No IDE-specific command files generated
- No .claude or .cursor directories created

**Method 2 (BMAD-integrated SKF - quick/):**
- Creates BOTH .claude/commands and .cursor/commands directories
- 21 command files total:
  - 11 SKF-specific commands (bmad-skf-*)
  - 1 SKF agent command (bmad-agent-skf-skf.md)
  - 9 core BMAD commands (bmad-brainstorming.md, bmad-help.md, etc.)

**Method 3 (BMAD core + SKF added later - deep/):**
- Creates BOTH .claude/commands and .cursor/commands directories
- Only 10 command files total (MISSING the 11 SKF-specific commands):

  - NO SKF workflow commands

**Method 1 (fast/):** None

**Method 2 (quick/) and Method 3 (deep/):**

```yaml
configured_date: 2026-03-08T06:54:04.116Z
last_updated: 2026-03-08T06:54:04.116Z
configuration:
  _noConfigNeeded: true
```

### CRITICAL INSIGHT: What Makes Method 2 Special

**Method 2 (BMAD-integrated SKF) generates these IDE-specific configurations that Method 1 does NOT:**

1. **Complete IDE command registration system** - Both `.claude/commands` and `.cursor/commands` with 21 discoverable commands
2. **BMAD configuration infrastructure** - `_bmad/_config/` directory with manifests, agent registry, and IDE-specific YAML files
3. **SKF customization integration** - `_bmad/_config/custom/skf/` directory for custom SKF overlays
4. **Dual IDE support** - Identical command trees in both Claude Code and Cursor via the ides/ configuration system
5. **Flat workflow structure** - SKF workflows not nested under /skillforge/, directly under /workflows/

**Method 3's limitation:** When SKF is added AFTER initial BMAD installation, it doesn't regenerate the IDE command files, so SKF workflows remain unavailable in the IDE command palette.

file.content:
agent:
  webskip: true
  metadata:
    id: "_bmad/skf/agents/skf.md"
    name: Ferris
    title: Skill Architect & Integrity Guardian
    icon: "⚒️"
    module: skf
    hasSidecar: true
    sidecar-folder: ferris-sidecar
    sidecar-path: "{project-root}/_bmad/_memory/ferris-sidecar/"

  persona:
    role: |
      Skill compilation specialist who transforms code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse into verified agent skills.
      Manages the full lifecycle: source analysis, skill briefing, AST-backed compilation,
      integrity testing, and ecosystem-ready export across progressive capability tiers
      (Quick/Forge/Deep).

    identity: |
      The forge master — a precision-focused craftsman who works through four modes:
      Architect (exploratory, assembling), Surgeon (precise, preserving), Audit
      (judgmental, scoring), and Delivery (packaging, ecosystem-ready). Modes are
      workflow-bound, not conversation-bound. Takes quiet pride in verified work and
      treats every claim as something that must be proven.

    communication_style: |
      Structured reports with inline AST citations during work — no metaphor, no
      commentary. At transitions, uses forge language: brief, warm, orienting. On
      completion, quiet craftsman's pride. On errors, direct and actionable with no
      hedging. Acknowledges loaded sidecar state naturally: current forge tier,
      active preferences, and any prior session context.

    principles:
      - "Channel expert source code analysis wisdom: draw upon deep knowledge of AST patterns, function signatures, type systems, and what separates verified skill definitions from hallucinated ones"
      - "Zero hallucination tolerance — every instruction traces to code; if it can't be cited, it doesn't exist"
      - "Structural truth over semantic guessing — AST first, always; never infer what can be parsed"
      - "Provenance is non-negotiable — every claim has a source, line number, and confidence tier"
      - "Meet developers where they are — progressive capability means Quick is legitimate, not lesser"
      - "Tools are backstage, the craft is center stage — users see results, not tool invocations"
      - "Agent-level knowledge informs judgment across workflows — consult knowledge/ when a step directs, not from memory"

  critical_actions:
    - "Load COMPLETE file {project-root}/_bmad/_memory/ferris-sidecar/preferences.yaml"
    - "Load COMPLETE file {project-root}/_bmad/_memory/ferris-sidecar/forge-tier.yaml"
    - "ONLY read/write files in {project-root}/_bmad/_memory/ferris-sidecar/"
    - "Consult {project-root}/_bmad/skf/knowledge/skf-knowledge-index.csv to select knowledge fragments under knowledge/ and load only the files needed for the current task"
    - "Load the referenced fragment(s) from {project-root}/_bmad/skf/knowledge/ before giving recommendations"

  menu:
    - trigger: SF or fuzzy match on setup-forge
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/setup-forge/workflow.md"
      description: "[SF] Setup Forge — initialize environment, detect tools, set tier"

    - trigger: AN or fuzzy match on analyze-source
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/analyze-source/workflow.md"
      description: "[AN] Analyze Source — discover what to skill, recommend stack skill"

    - trigger: BS or fuzzy match on brief-skill
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/brief-skill/workflow.md"
      description: "[BS] Brief Skill — design a skill scope through guided discovery"

    - trigger: CS or fuzzy match on create-skill
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/create-skill/workflow.md"
      description: "[CS] Create Skill — compile a skill from brief"

    - trigger: QS or fuzzy match on quick-skill
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/quick-skill/workflow.md"
      description: "[QS] Quick Skill — fast skill, no brief needed"

    - trigger: SS or fuzzy match on create-stack-skill
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/create-stack-skill/workflow.md"
      description: "[SS] Stack Skill — consolidated project stack skill"

    - trigger: US or fuzzy match on update-skill
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/update-skill/workflow.md"
      description: "[US] Update Skill — regenerate after changes, preserves [MANUAL]"

    - trigger: AS or fuzzy match on audit-skill
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/audit-skill/workflow.md"
      description: "[AS] Audit Skill — check for drift"

    - trigger: TS or fuzzy match on test-skill
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/test-skill/workflow.md"
      description: "[TS] Test Skill — verify completeness"

    - trigger: EX or fuzzy match on export-skill
      workflow: "{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/skillforge/export-skill/workflow.md"
      description: "[EX] Export Skill — package for distribution"

    - trigger: WS or fuzzy match on workflow-status
      action: "Show current lifecycle position, active skill briefs, and forge tier status"
      description: "[WS] Workflow Status — show current lifecycle position"

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Here's the analysis:

## Bug Summary

**The standalone SKF installer (Method 1) collects IDE selections but never generates IDE command files.**

### Root Cause

In `tools/cli/lib/installer.js`, the `install()` method runs 6 steps:
1. Copy source files
2. Setup sidecar
3. Write config.yaml
4. Compile agents
5. Create output folders
6. Copy learning material

**There is no Step 7 to generate IDE-specific command files.** The `config.ides` array (from user prompt) is saved to `config.yaml` but never acted upon.

### What's Missing vs. Method 2

Method 2 (BMAD-METHOD installer) generates these files that Method 1 doesn't:

| IDE | Directory | Files Generated |
|-----|-----------|----------------|
| Claude Code | `.claude/commands/` | 12 files (1 agent + 11 workflows) |
| Cursor | `.cursor/commands/` | 12 files (identical) |

The **12 SKF-specific command files** are:

**Agent command** (`bmad-agent-skf-skf.md`):
```markdown
---
name: 'skf'
description: 'skf agent'
---
<agent-activation> → loads _bmad/skf/agents/skf.md
```

**11 workflow commands** (e.g., `bmad-skf-analyze-source.md`):
```markdown
---
name: 'analyze-source'
description: 'Decomposition engine — discover what to skill...'
---
IT IS CRITICAL THAT YOU FOLLOW THIS COMMAND: LOAD the FULL
{project-root}/_bmad/skf/workflows/analyze-source/workflow.md...
```

### IDE Directory Mapping

From the BMAD-METHOD's `platform-codes.yaml`, each IDE needs files in:
- **Claude Code** → `.claude/commands/`  (flat `.md` files)

```markdown
IT IS CRITICAL THAT YOU FOLLOW THIS COMMAND: LOAD the FULL

READ its entire contents and follow its directions exactly!
```

file.content:
# BMAD Platform Codes Configuration
# Central configuration for all platform/IDE codes used in the BMAD system
#
# This file defines:
# 1. Platform metadata (name, preferred status, category, description)
# 2. Installer configuration (target directories, templates, artifact types)
#
# Format:
#   code: Platform identifier used internally
#   name: Display name shown to users
#   preferred: Whether this platform is shown as a recommended option on install
#   category: Type of platform (ide, cli, tool, service)
#   description: Brief description of the platform
#   installer: Installation configuration (optional - omit for custom installers)

platforms:
  antigravity:
    name: "Google Antigravity"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Google's AI development environment"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .agent/workflows
      target_dir: .agent/skills
      template_type: antigravity
      skill_format: true

  auggie:
    name: "Auggie"
    preferred: false
    category: cli
    description: "AI development tool"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .augment/commands
      target_dir: .augment/skills
      template_type: default
      skill_format: true

  claude-code:
    name: "Claude Code"
    preferred: true
    category: cli
    description: "Anthropic's official CLI for Claude"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .claude/commands
      target_dir: .claude/skills
      template_type: default
      skill_format: true
      ancestor_conflict_check: true

  cline:
    name: "Cline"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI coding assistant"
    installer:
      target_dir: .clinerules/workflows
      template_type: windsurf

  codex:
    name: "Codex"
    preferred: false
    category: cli
    description: "OpenAI Codex integration"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .codex/prompts
        - ~/.codex/prompts
      target_dir: .agents/skills
      template_type: default
      skill_format: true
      ancestor_conflict_check: true
      artifact_types: [agents, workflows, tasks]

  codebuddy:
    name: "CodeBuddy"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Tencent Cloud Code Assistant - AI-powered coding companion"
    installer:
      target_dir: .codebuddy/commands
      template_type: default

  crush:
    name: "Crush"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI development assistant"
    installer:
      target_dir: .crush/commands
      template_type: default

  cursor:
    name: "Cursor"
    preferred: true
    category: ide
    description: "AI-first code editor"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .cursor/commands
      target_dir: .cursor/skills
      template_type: default
      skill_format: true

  gemini:
    name: "Gemini CLI"
    preferred: false
    category: cli
    description: "Google's CLI for Gemini"
    installer:
      target_dir: .gemini/commands
      template_type: gemini

  github-copilot:
    name: "GitHub Copilot"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "GitHub's AI pair programmer"
    # No installer config - uses custom github-copilot.js

  iflow:
    name: "iFlow"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI workflow automation"
    installer:
      target_dir: .iflow/commands
      template_type: default

  kilo:
    name: "KiloCoder"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI coding platform"
    # No installer config - uses custom kilo.js (creates .kilocodemodes file)

  kiro:
    name: "Kiro"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Amazon's AI-powered IDE"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .kiro/steering
      target_dir: .kiro/skills
      template_type: kiro
      skill_format: true

  opencode:
    name: "OpenCode"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "OpenCode terminal coding assistant"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .opencode/agents
        - .opencode/commands
        - .opencode/agent
        - .opencode/command
      target_dir: .opencode/skills
      template_type: opencode
      skill_format: true
      ancestor_conflict_check: true

  qwen:
    name: "QwenCoder"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Qwen AI coding assistant"
    installer:
      target_dir: .qwen/commands
      template_type: default

  roo:
    name: "Roo Cline"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Enhanced Cline fork"
    installer:
      target_dir: .roo/commands
      template_type: default

  rovo-dev:
    name: "Rovo Dev"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "Atlassian's Rovo development environment"
    # No installer config - uses custom rovodev.js (generates prompts.yml manifest)

  trae:
    name: "Trae"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI coding tool"
    installer:
      target_dir: .trae/rules
      template_type: trae

  windsurf:
    name: "Windsurf"
    preferred: false
    category: ide
    description: "AI-powered IDE with cascade flows"
    installer:
      legacy_targets:
        - .windsurf/workflows
      target_dir: .windsurf/skills
      template_type: windsurf
      skill_format: true

# ============================================================================
# Installer Config Schema
# ============================================================================
#
# installer:
#   target_dir: string                    # Directory where artifacts are installed
#   template_type: string                 # Default template type to use
#   header_template: string (optional)    # Override for header/frontmatter template
#   body_template: string (optional)      # Override for body/content template
#   legacy_targets: array (optional)      # Old target dirs to clean up on reinstall (migration)
#     - string                            # Relative path, e.g. .opencode/agent
#   targets: array (optional)             # For multi-target installations
#     - target_dir: string
#       template_type: string
#       artifact_types: [agents, workflows, tasks, tools]
#   artifact_types: array (optional)      # Filter which artifacts to install (default: all)
#   skip_existing: boolean (optional)     # Skip files that already exist (default: false)
#   skill_format: boolean (optional)   # Use directory-per-skill output: <name>/SKILL.md
#                                     # with clean frontmatter (name + description, unquoted)
#   ancestor_conflict_check: boolean (optional) # Refuse install when ancestor dir has BMAD files
#                                                # in the same target_dir (for IDEs that inherit
#                                                # skills from parent directories)

# ============================================================================
# Platform Categories
# ============================================================================

categories:
  ide:
    name: "Integrated Development Environment"
    description: "Full-featured code editors with AI assistance"

  cli:
    name: "Command Line Interface"
    description: "Terminal-based tools"

  tool:
    name: "Development Tool"
    description: "Standalone development utilities"

  service:
    name: "Cloud Service"
    description: "Cloud-based development platforms"

  extension:
    name: "Editor Extension"
    description: "Plugins for existing editors"

# ============================================================================
# Naming Conventions and Rules
# ============================================================================

conventions:
  code_format: "lowercase-kebab-case"
  name_format: "Title Case"
  max_code_length: 20
  allowed_characters: "a-z0-9-"

content.text: The cwd was inside the deleted directory, but the cleanup succeeded. Let me verify the final state of the modified files:

Here's the summary of changes:

### Changes Made

1. **New file: `tools/cli/lib/ide-commands.js`** — IDE command generator that:
   - Maps 7 IDEs to their target directories (Claude Code, Cursor, Cline, Codex, GitHub Copilot, Roo, Windsurf)
   - Collects compiled agent files from `_bmad/skf/agents/`
   - Recursively finds `workflow.md` files and extracts frontmatter descriptions
   - Generates properly formatted agent activation commands and workflow launcher commands
   - Writes files with the `bmad-agent-skf-*` / `bmad-skf-*` naming convention

2. **Modified: `tools/cli/lib/installer.js`** — Added Step 7 after learning material:
   - Calls `generateIdeCommands()` with the selected IDEs from user prompts
   - Shows spinner with success/info status
   - Skips gracefully if only "other" is selected or no IDEs chosen

### Result

The standalone installer now generates **11 IDE command files** (1 agent + 10 workflows) for each selected IDE, matching what the BMAD-METHOD installer produces. Users selecting "Claude Code" and "Cursor" will get commands in `.claude/commands/` and `.cursor/commands/` respectively.

content.thinking:
The user is showing that re-installing works fine with the "Fresh install" option. But I think they're asking about a potential issue - let me think about what could go wrong:

1. When doing "Fresh install", `installer.js` removes the `_bmad/skf/` directory, but the IDE command files in `.claude/commands/` are NOT cleaned up. They'd just be overwritten (which is fine for the same IDE selections).

2. But what if on the first install they selected "Claude Code" AND "Cursor", and on the second install they only selected "Claude Code"? The `.cursor/commands/` files from the first install would remain as orphans.


3. The real issue is that stale IDE command files from previous installations don't get cleaned up when the user changes their IDE selections. The "Update" flow regenerates commands based on the saved config, but orphaned files from deselected IDEs still linger in their directories.

Looking back at the user's question, they're probably just asking what happens on reinstall and whether it works correctly. The output they showed looks fine—it detected the existing installation, offered the right options, and completed successfully with IDE commands generated. But I should dig into the reinstall logic more carefully to see if there are any edge cases, particularly around how sidecar files and IDE selections are handled across multiple installations.

Actually, I'm spotting a real issue here: when doing a fresh install, the code removes the `_bmad/skf/` directory but leaves behind any IDE command files from the previous installation. If the user previously had IDE commands set up for an IDE they're not selecting this time around, those orphaned files will just sit there. The installer should clean up old IDE command files before generating new ones for the currently selected IDEs.

content.text:
The output looks correct — reinstall works. But there's a subtle issue: **old IDE command files aren't cleaned up** when the user changes their IDE selection.

For example:
1. First install: select "Claude Code" + "Cursor" → files created in both `.claude/commands/` and `.cursor/commands/`
2. Reinstall (fresh): select only "Claude Code" → `.cursor/commands/bmad-skf-*` files remain as orphans

Let me fix this by cleaning up SKF-related IDE files before generating new ones.

content.content: The file /home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/tools/cli/lib/ide-commands.js has been updated successfully.

lines: + * Remove SKF-related command files from an IDE target directory.
+ * Only removes files matching the bmad-skf-* and bmad-agent-skf-* patterns.
+ * Remove stale SKF command files from all known IDE directories.
+ * Called before generating new commands to handle IDE selection changes.
* Generate IDE command files for all selected IDEs.
+  // Always clean old SKF commands from all IDEs first

content.text: Now I also need to handle the **Update** flow. Currently, when "Update" is chosen, the installer reads `config.ides` from the saved `config.yaml` but the IDE command step only runs if `config.ides` is present. Let me verify the update path works correctly.

Good — on update, `config.ides` is restored from saved `config.yaml` (line 37), so Step 7 will correctly regenerate IDE commands using the previously saved IDE selections. The cleanup handles stale files from any IDE.

The fix is complete. On reinstall:
- **Fresh install**: cleans old SKF commands from all IDE directories, then generates for newly selected IDEs
- **Update**: reads saved IDE list from `config.yaml`, cleans old commands, regenerates fresh ones

content.text: It works — all 11 command files are there in `.claude/commands/`. What specifically looked wrong to you? The output you shared shows a successful install with "IDE commands generated for claude-code".

message.content: we still have the .codex and .cursor commands. Is it normal?

content.text:
No, that's the stale files bug. The previous install selected Claude Code + Cursor, but this fresh install only selected Claude Code. The cleanup should have removed the old `.cursor/commands/bmad-skf-*` files.

Let me check what's actually there:

input.description: Check stale IDE files in fast/

content.content:
---CODEX---
prompts

toolUseResult.stdout:
---CODEX---
prompts

content.thinking:
So `.cursor/commands/` is empty (no output before `---CODEX---`), but `.codex/prompts/` exists. Wait, the cursor commands directory exists but has no files? Let me check more carefully.

Actually wait - the previous install was done with the OLD code before my changes. So the `.cursor/commands/` directory may have been created by a previous run where the user selected Cursor. Let me check if there are files in it.

Also `.codex/prompts/` - this wouldn't have been created by our installer since Codex wasn't in the user's selection. Unless... wait, the user said "we still have the .codex and .cursor commands." So those directories still exist from a previous install.
 
I see the problem now - the cleanup function iterates through each IDE target and removes matching files, but it's not deleting the empty directories that remain afterward. So even though the files get cleaned out, the directories themselves persist.

content.text: Now empty `.cursor/` and `.codex/` directories will be removed after their command files are cleaned. The cleanup walks up: removes the `commands/` dir if empty, then removes the `.cursor/` parent if also empty.

fix: clean stale IDE command files and empty directories on reinstall

their empty parent directories (e.g. .cursor/, .codex/) now get
removed before generating new ones.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && git status

    15→

    25→
    26→Follow the prompts to configure the module for your needs.
    27→
    28→---
    29→
    30→## Prerequisites
    31→
    32→| Tool                                                                   | Required For       | Install                     |
    33→|------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
    34→| `gh` (GitHub CLI)                                                      | All modes          | <https://cli.github.com>      |
    35→| `ast-grep`  (CLI tool for code structural search, lint, and rewriting) | Forge + Deep modes | <https://ast-grep.github.io>  |
    36→| `qmd` (local hybrid search engine for markdown)                        | Deep mode          | <https://github.com/tobi/qmd> |
    37→
    38→Don't worry if you don't have all tools — SKF detects what's available and sets your tier automatically.
    39→
    40→---
    41→
    42→## First Steps
    43→
    44→### 1. Setup Your Forge
    45→
    46→```
    47→@Ferris SF
    48→```
    49→
    50→This detects your tools, sets your capability tier, and initializes the forge environment. You only need to do this once per project.
    51→
    52→### 2. Generate Your First Skill
    53→
    54→**Fastest path (Quick Skill):**
    55→```
    56→@Ferris QS https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD
    57→```
    58→
    59→Ferris reads the repository, extracts the public API, and generates a skill in under a minute.
    60→
    61→**Full quality path:**
    62→```
    63→@Ferris BS    # Brief — scope and design the skill
    64→@Ferris CS    # Create — compile from the brief
    65→@Ferris TS    # Test — verify completeness
    66→@Ferris EX    # Export — package for distribution
    67→```
    68→
    69→### 3. Stack Skill (for full projects)
    70→
    71→```
    72→@Ferris SS
    73→```
    74→
    75→Analyzes your project's dependencies and generates a consolidated stack skill with integration patterns.
    76→
    77→---
    78→
    79→## Common Use Cases
    80→
    81→### My agent keeps hallucinating API calls
    82→
    83→Your agent invents function signatures that don't exist. Generate a verified skill so it works from structural truth instead of guessing.
    84→
    85→```
    86→@Ferris QS https://github.com/org/library
    87→```
    88→
    89→The skill pins every function to its actual source location. Hallucinations stop.
    90→
    91→### I'm adopting a new library and need my agent to use it correctly
    92→
    93→You added a dependency but your agent doesn't know its API yet. Quick Skill resolves package names across npm, PyPI, and crates.io.
    94→
    95→```
    96→@Ferris QS cognee
    97→```
    98→
    99→Ferris resolves the package to its GitHub repo, extracts the public API, and generates a skill your agent can reference immediately.
   100→
   101→### I want my agent to understand my entire project stack
   102→
   103→Individual skills cover single libraries. Stack Skill maps how your dependencies interact — shared types, co-import patterns, integration points.
   104→
   105→```
   106→@Ferris SS
   107→```
   108→
   109→Ferris detects your manifests, ranks dependencies by significance, and generates a consolidated skill with cross-library integration patterns.
   110→
   111→### I'm onboarding a large existing codebase
   112→
   113→A brownfield repo with dozens of modules. You need to know what's worth skilling before you start.
   114→
   115→```
   116→@Ferris AN
   117→```
   118→
   119→Analyze Source scans the project, identifies skillable units, maps exports, and generates recommended briefs you can batch-create with `@Ferris CS --batch`.
   120→
   121→### I want the highest accuracy possible
   122→
   123→Quick mode reads source files. Forge mode adds AST parsing for structural truth. Deep mode goes further — QMD indexes your project's markdown (docs, changelogs, issues, migration guides) into searchable collections, then uses BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM-powered re-ranking to surface context no other tool can find.
   124→
   125→```
   126→@Ferris SF    # Setup detects your tools and sets tier automatically
   127→```
   128→
   129→If `ast-grep` and `qmd` are available, Ferris activates Deep mode. Every skill gets AST-verified signatures (T1) enriched with deep knowledge search (T2) — deprecation warnings, breaking change history, common pitfalls surfaced by hybrid search across your indexed documentation.
   130→
   131→### I maintain an OSS library and want official skills
   132→
   133→You want maximum quality with full provenance for distribution. Use the full pipeline with Deep mode for the richest output.
   134→
   135→```
   136→@Ferris BS    # Scope and design the skill
   137→@Ferris CS    # Compile — AST extraction + QMD enrichment (Deep)
   138→@Ferris TS    # Verify completeness before publishing
   139→@Ferris EX    # Package for distribution
   140→```
   141→
   142→Export with `source_authority: official`. Consumers get verified skills that update with each release.
   143→
   144→### A dependency shipped breaking changes
   145→
   146→Your existing skill is now out of date. Audit detects the drift, Update regenerates while preserving your manual additions.
   147→
   148→```
   149→@Ferris AS    # Find what changed
   150→@Ferris US    # Regenerate — [MANUAL] sections survive
   151→@Ferris TS    # Verify the update
   152→```
   153→
   154→---
   155→
   156→## What's Next?
   157→
   158→- Check out the [Agents Reference](../agents.md) to learn about Ferris
   159→- Browse the [Workflows Reference](../workflows.md) to see all available commands
   160→- See [Examples](../examples.md) for real-world usage scenarios
   161→
   162→---
   163→
   164→## Need Help?
   165→
   166→If you run into issues:
   167→1. Run `/bmad-help` — analyzes your current state and suggests what to do next
   168→   (e.g. `/bmad-help my quick skill has low confidence scores, how do I improve them?`)
   169→2. Run `@Ferris SF` to check your tool availability and tier
   170→3. Check `forge-config.yaml` for your current configuration
   171→

---
title: Getting Started
description: Installation, prerequisites, first steps, and common use cases for Skill Forge
---

# Getting Started with SKF: Skill Forge

Welcome to Skill Forge! This guide will help you get up and running.

---

## What This Module Does

Skill Forge is an automated skill compiler for the AI agent ecosystem. It transforms code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse into agentskills.io-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every instruction traces to actual code — zero hallucination tolerance.

---

## Installation

If you haven't installed the module yet:

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Follow the prompts to configure the module for your needs.

---

## Prerequisites

| Tool                                                                   | Required For       | Install                     |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| `gh` (GitHub CLI)                                                      | All modes          | <https://cli.github.com>      |
| `ast-grep`  (CLI tool for code structural search, lint, and rewriting) | Forge + Deep modes | <https://ast-grep.github.io>  |
| `qmd` (local hybrid search engine for markdown)                        | Deep mode          | <https://github.com/tobi/qmd> |

Don't worry if you don't have all tools — SKF detects what's available and sets your tier automatically.

---

## First Steps

### 1. Setup Your Forge

```
@Ferris SF
```

This detects your tools, sets your capability tier, and initializes the forge environment. You only need to do this once per project.

### 2. Generate Your First Skill

**Fastest path (Quick Skill):**
```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD
```

Ferris reads the repository, extracts the public API, and generates a skill in under a minute.

**Full quality path:**
```
@Ferris BS    # Brief — scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Create — compile from the brief
@Ferris TS    # Test — verify completeness
@Ferris EX    # Export — package for distribution
```

### 3. Stack Skill (for full projects)

```
@Ferris SS
```

Analyzes your project's dependencies and generates a consolidated stack skill with integration patterns.

---

## Common Use Cases

### My agent keeps hallucinating API calls

Your agent invents function signatures that don't exist. Generate a verified skill so it works from structural truth instead of guessing.

```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/org/library
```

The skill pins every function to its actual source location. Hallucinations stop.

### I'm adopting a new library and need my agent to use it correctly

You added a dependency but your agent doesn't know its API yet. Quick Skill resolves package names across npm, PyPI, and crates.io.

```
@Ferris QS cognee
```

Ferris resolves the package to its GitHub repo, extracts the public API, and generates a skill your agent can reference immediately.

### I want my agent to understand my entire project stack

Individual skills cover single libraries. Stack Skill maps how your dependencies interact — shared types, co-import patterns, integration points.

```
@Ferris SS
```

Ferris detects your manifests, ranks dependencies by significance, and generates a consolidated skill with cross-library integration patterns.

### I'm onboarding a large existing codebase

A brownfield repo with dozens of modules. You need to know what's worth skilling before you start.

```
@Ferris AN
```

Analyze Source scans the project, identifies skillable units, maps exports, and generates recommended briefs you can batch-create with `@Ferris CS --batch`.

### I want the highest accuracy possible

Quick mode reads source files. Forge mode adds AST parsing for structural truth. Deep mode goes further — QMD indexes your project's markdown (docs, changelogs, issues, migration guides) into searchable collections, then uses BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM-powered re-ranking to surface context no other tool can find.

```
@Ferris SF    # Setup detects your tools and sets tier automatically
```

If `ast-grep` and `qmd` are available, Ferris activates Deep mode. Every skill gets AST-verified signatures (T1) enriched with deep knowledge search (T2) — deprecation warnings, breaking change history, common pitfalls surfaced by hybrid search across your indexed documentation.

### I maintain an OSS library and want official skills

You want maximum quality with full provenance for distribution. Use the full pipeline with Deep mode for the richest output.

```
@Ferris BS    # Scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Compile — AST extraction + QMD enrichment (Deep)
@Ferris TS    # Verify completeness before publishing
@Ferris EX    # Package for distribution
```

Export with `source_authority: official`. Consumers get verified skills that update with each release.

### A dependency shipped breaking changes

Your existing skill is now out of date. Audit detects the drift, Update regenerates while preserving your manual additions.

```
@Ferris AS    # Find what changed
@Ferris US    # Regenerate — [MANUAL] sections survive
@Ferris TS    # Verify the update
```

---

## What's Next?

- Check out the [Agents Reference](../agents.md) to learn about Ferris
- Browse the [Workflows Reference](../workflows.md) to see all available commands
- See [Examples](../examples.md) for real-world usage scenarios

---

## Need Help?

If you run into issues:
1. Run `/bmad-help` — analyzes your current state and suggests what to do next
   (e.g. `/bmad-help my quick skill has low confidence scores, how do I improve them?`)
2. Run `@Ferris SF` to check your tool availability and tier
3. Check `forge-config.yaml` for your current configuration

title: Skill Forge (SKF)
description: AST-verified, provenance-backed agent skills from code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse
template: splash
hero:
  title: Skill Forge (SKF)
  tagline: AST-verified, provenance-backed agent skills from code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse
  actions:
    - text: Getting Started
      link: ./getting-started/

## What is Skill Forge?

Skill Forge is an automated skill compiler for the AI agent ecosystem. It transforms code repositories, documentation, and developer discourse into [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io)-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every instruction traces to verifiable sources — zero hallucination tolerance.

- **AST-Verified**: Structural truth via ast-grep — no guessing, no hallucination.
- **Provenance-Backed**: Every claim traces to source code with file and line references.
- **Progressive Tiers**: Quick (no setup) → Forge (ast-grep) → Deep (QMD knowledge).
- **Ecosystem-First**: Checks for official skills before generating community ones.

## Quick Install

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Then interact with the forge agent:

```
@Ferris SF    # Setup your forge environment
@Ferris QS    # Quick Skill — generate in under a minute
```

## Core Workflows

| Workflow | Trigger | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| [Setup Forge](/workflows/#setup-forge-sf) | SF | Initialize forge, detect tools, set tier |
| [Brief Skill](/workflows/#brief-skill-bs) | BS | Scope and design a skill |
| [Create Skill](/workflows/#create-skill-cs) | CS | Compile a skill from a brief |
| [Quick Skill](/workflows/#quick-skill-qs) | QS | Fast skill, no brief needed |
| [Stack Skill](/workflows/#stack-skill-ss) | SS | Consolidated project stack skill |
| [Update Skill](/workflows/#update-skill-us) | US | Regenerate after source changes |
| [Audit Skill](/workflows/#audit-skill-as) | AS | Drift detection |
| [Test Skill](/workflows/#test-skill-ts) | TS | Verify completeness |
| [Export Skill](/workflows/#export-skill-ex) | EX | Package for distribution |
| [Analyze Source](/workflows/#analyze-source-an) | AN | Discover what to skill |

## Documentation

- **[Getting Started](./getting-started.md)** — Installation, prerequisites, first steps
- **[Architecture](./architecture.md)** — Output format, confidence model, tiers, tool ecosystem
- **[Agents](./agents.md)** — Ferris agent: modes, capabilities, communication style
- **[Workflows](./workflows.md)** — All 10 workflows with commands and connection diagram
- **[Examples](./examples.md)** — Real-world scenarios, tips, and troubleshooting

## Support

- **Issues**: [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/armelhbobdad/bmad-module-skill-forge/issues)

---
title: Getting Started
description: Installation, prerequisites, first steps, and common use cases for Skill Forge
---

# Getting Started with SKF: Skill Forge

Welcome to Skill Forge! This guide will help you get up and running.

---

## What This Module Does

Skill Forge is an automated skill compiler for the AI agent ecosystem. It transforms code repositories, documentation websites, and developer discourse into agentskills.io-compliant, version-pinned, provenance-backed agent skills. Every instruction traces to actual code — zero hallucination tolerance.

---

## Installation

If you haven't installed the module yet:

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Follow the prompts to configure the module for your needs.

---

## Prerequisites

| Tool                                                                   | Required For       | Install                     |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| `gh` (GitHub CLI)                                                      | All modes          | <https://cli.github.com>      |
| `ast-grep`  (CLI tool for code structural search, lint, and rewriting) | Forge + Deep modes | <https://ast-grep.github.io>  |
| `qmd` (local hybrid search engine for markdown)                        | Deep mode          | <https://github.com/tobi/qmd> |

Don't worry if you don't have all tools — SKF detects what's available and sets your tier automatically.

---

## First Steps

### 1. Setup Your Forge

```
@Ferris SF
```

This detects your tools, sets your capability tier, and initializes the forge environment. You only need to do this once per project.

### 2. Generate Your First Skill

**Fastest path (Quick Skill):**
```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/bmad-code-org/BMAD-METHOD
```

Ferris reads the repository, extracts the public API, and generates a skill in under a minute.

**Full quality path:**
```
@Ferris BS    # Brief — scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Create — compile from the brief
@Ferris TS    # Test — verify completeness
@Ferris EX    # Export — package for distribution
```

### 3. Stack Skill (for full projects)

```
@Ferris SS
```

Analyzes your project's dependencies and generates a consolidated stack skill with integration patterns.

---

## Common Use Cases

### My agent keeps hallucinating API calls

Your agent invents function signatures that don't exist. Generate a verified skill so it works from structural truth instead of guessing.

```
@Ferris QS https://github.com/org/library
```

The skill pins every function to its actual source location. Hallucinations stop.

### I'm adopting a new library and need my agent to use it correctly

You added a dependency but your agent doesn't know its API yet. Quick Skill resolves package names across npm, PyPI, and crates.io.

```
@Ferris QS cognee
```

Ferris resolves the package to its GitHub repo, extracts the public API, and generates a skill your agent can reference immediately.

### I want my agent to understand my entire project stack

Individual skills cover single libraries. Stack Skill maps how your dependencies interact — shared types, co-import patterns, integration points.

```
@Ferris SS
```

Ferris detects your manifests, ranks dependencies by significance, and generates a consolidated skill with cross-library integration patterns.

### I'm onboarding a large existing codebase

A brownfield repo with dozens of modules. You need to know what's worth skilling before you start.

```
@Ferris AN
```

Analyze Source scans the project, identifies skillable units, maps exports, and generates recommended briefs you can batch-create with `@Ferris CS --batch`.

### I want the highest accuracy possible

Quick mode reads source files. Forge mode adds AST parsing for structural truth. Deep mode goes further — QMD indexes your project's markdown (docs, changelogs, issues, migration guides) into searchable collections, then uses BM25 keyword search, vector semantic search, and LLM-powered re-ranking to surface context no other tool can find.

```
@Ferris SF    # Setup detects your tools and sets tier automatically
```

If `ast-grep` and `qmd` are available, Ferris activates Deep mode. Every skill gets AST-verified signatures (T1) enriched with deep knowledge search (T2) — deprecation warnings, breaking change history, common pitfalls surfaced by hybrid search across your indexed documentation.

### I maintain an OSS library and want official skills

You want maximum quality with full provenance for distribution. Use the full pipeline with Deep mode for the richest output.

```
@Ferris BS    # Scope and design the skill
@Ferris CS    # Compile — AST extraction + QMD enrichment (Deep)
@Ferris TS    # Verify completeness before publishing
@Ferris EX    # Package for distribution
```

Export with `source_authority: official`. Consumers get verified skills that update with each release.

### A dependency shipped breaking changes

Your existing skill is now out of date. Audit detects the drift, Update regenerates while preserving your manual additions.

```
@Ferris AS    # Find what changed
@Ferris US    # Regenerate — [MANUAL] sections survive
@Ferris TS    # Verify the update
```

---

## What's Next?

- Check out the [Agents Reference](../agents.md) to learn about Ferris
- Browse the [Workflows Reference](../workflows.md) to see all available commands
- See [Examples](../examples.md) for real-world usage scenarios

---

## Need Help?

If you run into issues:
1. Run `/bmad-help` — analyzes your current state and suggests what to do next
   (e.g. `/bmad-help my quick skill has low confidence scores, how do I improve them?`)
2. Run `@Ferris SF` to check your tool availability and tier
3. Check `forge-config.yaml` for your current configuration

## Install

There are three ways to install SKF, depending on your setup.

### Method 1: Standalone (recommended for trying SKF)

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

Installs SKF on its own. You'll be prompted for project name, output folders, and which IDEs to configure. The installer generates IDE-specific command files (e.g. `.claude/commands/`, `.cursor/commands/`) so workflows appear in your IDE's command palette.

### Method 2: With BMad Method (recommended for full workflow)

```bash
npx bmad-method install
```

During module selection, choose **SKF: Skill Forge**. This installs BMad core + SKF together with full IDE integration, manifests, and help catalog. Best when you want the complete BMad development workflow.

### Method 3: Add SKF to an existing BMad project

If you already have BMad installed via `npx bmad-method install`, you can add SKF afterward by running the standalone installer in the same directory:

```bash
npx bmad-module-skill-forge install
```

The installer detects the existing `_bmad/` directory and installs SKF alongside your current modules. IDE command files are generated for SKF workflows.
